title: GitHub forking and syncing
description: How to sync your fork on GitHub with the original repository
author: Simon Scholz

children.value:
When you intent to contribute to a project, which is not owned by you or no one has given you write access to the repository, you'd usually do this via forks.
Basically forking a repository means that you're copying the repository to your account so that you can make changes to it.
After the changes are done you may create a PR (pull request) against the original repository.
In order to create a fork you can navigate to this repository and hit the fork button:
When clicking the fork button you should see a similar dialog, where you can choose where to copy the repository.
Once this has been done you can clone the forked repository, change it according to your needs and also provide a PR for the original repository.

Of course your fork belongs to you and therefore will remain unchanged while the original repository evolves.
To catch up with the changes you might want to sync your own repository with the original repository`s master branch.
Add the original repository as remote

props.className: language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-dark monokai
props.code: git remote -v

children.value: remote, which points to your forked repository.
To also get (read) access to the original repository an upstream remote can be added:

props.className: language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-dark monokai
props.code:
git remote add upstream https://github.com/ORIGINAL_OWNER/ORIGINAL_REPOSITORY.git

e.g.

git remote add upstream git@github.com:gradle/gradle.git

children.value: is invoked once again there should be an
Sync fork with the upstream master

To get the latest changes from the upstream remote you checkout the master branch (usually left untouched, because your changes are in feature or bugfix branches) and then fetch + merge the changes from the upstream master branch.

props.className: language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-dark monokai
props.code:
git checkout master
git fetch upstream
git merge upstream/master
git push origin master

children.value: After merging the changes from the upstream remote your own master branch is even with the upstream.

title: PDF Tools
description: Tutorial about tools for editing PDF files.
author: Simon Scholz

children.value:
It also depends on the PDF file itself whether one of these tools properly visualizes the original PDF file.
So depending on the PDF files you might choose one tool over another.
Recently I've been using Inkscape, because LibreOffice Draw tends to screw the text alignment of a PDF files sometimes.
A drawback of using Inkscape is that it is only capable to open one page at a time.
So in case you want to modify several pages with Inkscape, you'll have to do it one by one and use tools like PDF Arranger to merge the resulting PDFs afterwards again.
Since many forms have between 2 or 3 pages it might be worth the effort.
Xournal++ is an open-source and cross-platform note-taking software that is fast, flexible, and functional. A modern rewrite and a more feature-rich version of the wonderful Xournal program.
You can also easily modify and create PDF files with it.

title: Using Docker
description: Tutorial about Docker concerning containers and networking.
author: Simon Scholz

children.value: Docker can be used to package software bundles inside so called containers. Docker has a similarity with virtual machines, but it consumes less resources since containers run on a single OS kernel and therefore also boot up way faster than a virtual machine would do.
A really popular resource to obtain production ready predefined containers is

. There you can find sample containers for many different software bundles. It is often easier to run certain software inside a docker container instead of directly installing the software on your local machine, because containers are easy manageable and encapsulated from the host system.

Wanna try out some software without bothering about the dependencies and configuration of this piece of software + dependencies? Then Docker can also help.
Let's take Drupal (nice cms api server, which I sometimes use for my Gatsby websites) as an example.
By simply running
) a Drupal Docker image will be pulled from Docker hub and started right away and will out of the box come with a PHP server, SQLite database and you can simply try Drupal by looking up
Of course docker can do way more and even bigger frameworks like Kubernetes and other utilize Docker as well.
A good and official description on how to install Docker on your operating system can be found here:
For Ubuntu I had to enter the following commands inside a terminal:

props.className: language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-dark monokai
props.code:
sudo apt-get update

sudo apt-get install \
    apt-transport-https \
    ca-certificates \
    curl \
    gnupg-agent \
    software-properties-common

curl -fsSL https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu/gpg | sudo apt-key add -

sudo add-apt-repository \
   "deb [arch=amd64] https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu \
   $(lsb_release -cs) \
   stable"

sudo apt-get update

sudo apt-get install docker-ce docker-ce-cli containerd.io

children.value: Run docker container and immediately remove it again

When you're creating docker images for testing purposes or an image that's supposed to do a job only once, you'd usually want to remove it after running immediately.
This can be archived by passing the

props.className: language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-dark monokai
props.code: docker run --rm my-run-only-once-docker-image

children.value:
This will run the respective docker image and remove the container once it exits.
This can be really handy when experimenting with docker images, which you created on your own.
you also get the ids of the container, which can be used to stop a certain container like this:

props.className: language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-dark monokai
props.code: docker stop <your-desired-container-id>

children.value: To stop all running container the following command can be used:

props.className: language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-dark monokai
props.code: docker stop $(docker ps -aq)

props.className: language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-dark monokai
props.code: docker rm <your-desired-container-id>

children.value: So removing all containers would look like this:

props.className: language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-dark monokai
props.code: docker rm $(docker ps -aq)
